literal: putting-together, collectedness, meditative absorption; from sam- ('together') + ā- ('toward') + √dhā 'to place, to set down' (sam-ā-√dhā + ki → samādhi), 'a placing-together' of the scattered faculties
invariant_definition: *Samādhi* is the gathered, composed condition of the mind in which attention is collected upon a single object or, in the highest forms, rests without an object in undivided awareness. The etymology supports this: a 'placing-together' of the otherwise scattered cognitive faculties. Beyond this shared core the term carries distinct technical senses. In Pātañjala Yoga it is the eighth and final limb of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* (YS 2.29) and the title of the first *pāda*; it is analyzed into *samprajñāta* (cognitive, object-bearing absorption attended by *vitarka, vicāra, ānanda, asmitā*, YS 1.17) and *asamprajñāta* (the absorption following the cessation of cognitive content, in which only latent impressions remain, YS 1.18), and it is treated as the proximate means to *kaivalya*. In Buddhist usage *samādhi* is the second of the three trainings (*sīla–samādhi–paññā*) and the concentration cultivated through the *jhānas*, which steadies the mind so that liberating insight (*paññā*) may arise. In Advaita the term is adopted and subdivided into *savikalpa* and *nirvikalpa samādhi*, and its soteriological status is debated — whether *nirvikalpa samādhi* is necessary for, identical with, or merely auxiliary to *brahma-jñāna*. One side of the debate holds that *samādhi* is a temporary state that ends when one rises from it, whereas liberating knowledge, once arisen, is held to be permanent. The term thus stands at the intersection of a yogic analysis of absorption, a Buddhist discipline of concentration, and the Vedāntic question of whether absorption is the same as knowledge of the real.

school_framing.shared_core: *Samādhi* names **the collected, undistracted condition of the mind gathered upon its object or resting objectless in undivided awareness**. The structural role — culminating concentration — is widely shared. Schools disagree on whether it is the technical eighth limb yielding *kaivalya* (Yoga), the concentration that supports but does not itself constitute liberating insight (Buddhism, and much of Advaita), or the steadiness of the equanimous intellect (the Gītā's looser use).
school_framing.register_axes_note: **Real disagreement** within Advaita and against the absorption-centred reading over the status of object-less absorption: whether *nirvikalpa samādhi* is required for or identical with *brahma-jñāna*, or whether knowledge, held to be permanent and self-revealing, does not depend on a temporary mental state at all. **Same concept, different aspect** between Yoga's technical eighth-limb *samādhi*, Buddhist concentration, and the Gītā's *samādhi* as steadiness of *buddhi*: all name mental collectedness at different degrees of technical specification.

per_school.definition: *Samādhi* is the eighth limb of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* and the consummation of *saṃyama* (the triad *dhāraṇā–dhyāna–samādhi*, YS 3.1–4); in *samādhi* the object alone shines forth as if emptied of the cognizing act's own form (YS 3.3). It is divided into *samprajñāta*, object-bearing absorption attended by reasoning, reflection, bliss, and I-sense (YS 1.17), and *asamprajñāta*, the higher absorption following the cessation of all cognitive content, leaving only latent impressions (YS 1.18). It is the direct means by which the *vṛttis* are stilled and *vivekakhyāti* ripens toward *kaivalya*; the whole first *pāda* is the *samādhi-pāda*.
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; yogin (eighth limb, means to kaivalya)

per_school.definition: Advaita adopts *samādhi* and divides it into *savikalpa* (absorption still bearing the subtle distinction of knower, known, and knowing) and *nirvikalpa* (absorption free of that triadic distinction). Its soteriological status is debated within the school: one strand treats *nirvikalpa samādhi* as a discipline for steadying the mind so that *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* and *brahma-jñāna* may arise; another holds that liberation is by knowledge alone (*jñānād eva mokṣaḥ*) and that *samādhi*, as a state one enters and leaves, cannot itself be liberation, since *brahma-jñāna* once arisen is held to be permanent and unaffected by the return to ordinary cognition. The point at issue is the temporariness of any absorbed state set against the claimed permanence of realized identity.
primary_loci: Pañcadaśī (the status of nirvikalpa samādhi vis-à-vis jñāna)
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu [REAL-DISAGREEMENT: nirvikalpa samādhi vs jñāna]

per_school.definition: *Samādhi* is the second of the three trainings (*sīla–samādhi–paññā*) and the right concentration (*sammā-samādhi*) of the eightfold path, cultivated through the four *jhānas* of progressively unified, equanimous absorption. It is the indispensable support of insight: concentration steadies and purifies the mind, but it is *paññā* (wisdom) that sees and uproots ignorance. Concentration alone, on the classical analysis, suppresses the hindrances and yields meditative attainment but does not by itself effect liberation.
primary_loci: the three sikkhā (sīla-samādhi-paññā) and the eightfold path (sammā-samādhi)
the four jhānas in the suttas
per_school.register_tag: Φ+S; reflective reader (concentration as support of paññā)

per_school.definition: In a looser, non-technical sense *samādhi* names the intellect rendered steady and unshaken, fixed in equanimity — the *buddhi* established and immovable rather than a graded technology of absorptive states. The accent falls on equanimity and the withdrawal of the intellect from the pull of competing sense-impressions; this usage feeds the *Gītā*'s wider identification of yoga with evenness of mind (*samatvaṃ yoga ucyate*, 2.48).
per_school.register_tag: M+S; mumukṣu (samādhi as steadiness of buddhi)

textual_history.summary: *Samādhi* (sam- 'together' + ā- 'toward' + √dhā 'to place') means literally a 'placing-together' — a gathering of the scattered faculties. Unlike *yoga* and *dharma*, the noun has no prominent Vedic Saṃhitā career in the technical sense; its philosophical life begins when the Upaniṣadic vocabulary of the *collected* and *quieted* mind (the senses brought to stillness, the intellect not stirring) is given a name. The Kaṭha already describes the state — 'when the five senses stand still together with the mind, and the intellect does not stir' (Kaṭha 2.3.10) — without yet using the word. Patañjali then makes *samādhi* a precise term: the eighth and culminating limb of *aṣṭāṅga-yoga* (YS 2.29), in which 'the object alone shines forth, as if emptied of [the act's] own form' (YS 3.3), graded into *samprajñāta* (object-bearing, cognitive) and *asamprajñāta* (objectless, with only latent impressions remaining) (YS 1.17–18). The *Gītā*, roughly contemporary, uses the word more loosely for the *steadied intellect* — the *buddhi* grown immovable (2.53, *samādhāv acalā buddhiḥ*), the equanimous mind no longer scattered by competing sense-pulls (2.44). The later Advaita appropriation (*savikalpa*/*nirvikalpa samādhi*) and its dispute over whether objectless absorption is identical with, or merely auxiliary to, liberating knowledge belongs to the post-classical commentarial period and is treated in the per-school material, not here. The development is largely genealogical for the technical sense (Upaniṣadic stilled mind → Pātañjala eighth limb) and a parallel, looser usage in the *Gītā*; we mark the Yoga-vs-Advaita reading divergence without adjudicating it.

stages.sanskrit: yadā pañcāvatiṣṭhante jñānāni manasā saha | buddhiś ca na viceṣṭati tām āhuḥ paramāṃ gatim
stages.rendering: When the five knowledges (the senses) stand still together with the mind, and the intellect (buddhi) does not stir — that they call the highest course.
stages.note: The state later named *samādhi* is described before the word is technicalised: the senses and mind brought to a standstill, the intellect grown still. The verb *avatiṣṭhante* ('stand still together') is itself close to the sense of *sam-ā-√dhā*, a placing-together of the faculties. This is the conceptual seed; the Upaniṣad here calls the result *yoga* (next verse, 2.3.11), not yet *samādhi*.

stages.sanskrit: deśabandhaś cittasya dhāraṇā || tatra pratyayaikatānatā dhyānam || tad evārthamātranirbhāsaṃ svarūpaśūnyam iva samādhiḥ
stages.rendering: The binding of the mind-stuff to a place is concentration (dhāraṇā). The one-pointed flow of a single notion there is meditation (dhyāna). That same [meditation], shining forth as the object alone, as if emptied of its own form, is absorption (samādhi).
stages.note: Patañjali's structural definition: *samādhi* is the consummation of the inner triad — concentration deepening into meditation, meditation into an absorption so complete that the cognising act's own form 'as it were' drops away and only the object shines (*arthamātra-nirbhāsa*). The three together form *saṃyama* (YS 3.4). This is the technical sense the Upaniṣadic 'stilled mind' has now become.

stages.rendering: [Absorption] is 'with-cognition' (saṃprajñāta) when accompanied by [the forms of] reasoning, reflection, bliss, and I-am-ness. || The other [absorption], preceded by the practice of the notion of cessation, has [only] latent impressions remaining.
stages.note: The internal analysis: *samprajñāta* absorption still bears cognitive content (reasoning, reflection, bliss, the sense of 'I am'); *asamprajñāta* (here 'the other', *anyaḥ*) is what remains when even that ceases, leaving only the *saṃskāras* — the latent impressions. This grading is Patañjali's distinctive contribution and the proximate technology toward *kaivalya*; the *Gītā*'s usage knows nothing so articulated.

stages.sanskrit: bhogaiśvarya-prasaktānāṃ tayāpahṛta-cetasām | vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ samādhau na vidhīyate
stages.rendering: For those attached to enjoyment and lordship, whose minds are carried off by that [flowery speech], the resolute intellect (vyavasāyātmikā buddhiḥ) is not established in samādhi.
stages.note: The *Gītā*'s first use, and already non-technical: *samādhi* is not a graded absorptive state but the condition of the *settled, resolute intellect* — and those distracted by pleasure and power cannot fix the *buddhi* there. The accent falls on single-minded steadiness against scattering, not on Patañjali's object-emptied shining.

stages.sanskrit: śruti-vipratipannā te yadā sthāsyati niścalā | samādhāv acalā buddhis tadā yogam avāpsyasi
stages.rendering: When your intellect, [now] bewildered by what is heard [of the Vedas], shall stand unmoving, motionless in samādhi — then you will attain yoga.
stages.note: *Samādhi* here is glossed by Śaṅkara as the self/mind in which the intellect is gathered — 'samādhīyate cittam asmin iti samādhiḥ', 'that in which the mind is placed-together is samādhi' — and the goal is the *unmoving buddhi*. The accent is again steadiness, feeding the *Gītā*'s identification of yoga with equanimity (2.48). (Our verbatim source, the on-disk Śaṅkara Gītā-Bhāṣya, prints the OCR error 'avāpsyasyi' for the verse's 'avāpsyasi' and 'vipratinnā/pratinnā' in the gloss for 'vipratipannā/pratipannā'; we print the standard readings and flag the source slips.)

translator_note: 'Trance' is misleading and 'meditation' too broad. In Yoga *samādhi* is a precise technical stage (the eighth limb), not meditation in general; in Buddhism it is the concentration-training distinguished from insight; in Advaita it names savikalpa/nirvikalpa absorption whose relation to liberating knowledge is itself contested. The English should not imply that absorption is automatically liberation — much of Advaita denies exactly that, holding that knowledge, not the temporary absorbed state, is what frees, and the Buddhist analysis likewise subordinates concentration to wisdom.
